prompt: An AI PR-reviewer sends a 6,000-token prompt (diff + style guide) and receives a 200-token review. Input is priced at $5 per million tokens and output at $15 per million tokens. Roughly what does one review cost?
choices: About $0.033
About $0.093
About $0.123
About $0.005

prompt: Which of the following typically INCREASE the latency of an AI check running in CI on each pull request? (Select all that apply.)
choices: Using a larger model for the review
Asking for a longer, more detailed review
Fetching extra source files before the model answers
Streaming the comments to the developer as they are generated

prompt: A team is optimizing an AI assistant that runs in their pipeline. Which statements about its cost and latency are correct? (Select all that apply.)
choices: Prefix-caching the style guide avoids re-paying to process it on every PR
Streaming reduces the total number of tokens generated per review
Routing tiny diffs to a smaller model can cut both cost and latency
Output tokens (the review comments) are usually priced higher than input tokens

prompt: A PM is told the AI merge-gate check feels slow to developers even though its total time is acceptable for CI, and the tooling budget is tight. Which change best improves PERCEIVED speed WITHOUT increasing cost?
choices: Switch every PR to a larger, more capable model
Stream the review so comments appear as they are generated
Remove prefix caching to simplify the pipeline
Raise the maximum review length

prompt: Caching responses cuts cost and latency but can serve stale answers. For a feature answering questions over data that changes every hour, which caching *trade-off* is appropriate?
choices: Aggressively cache every response for as long as possible
Use a short TTL (or cache only the stable parts) so answers stay fresh enough for hourly-changing data
Never cache anything anywhere, to be safe
Cache by user rather than by query so everyone gets their own stale copy
